bootstrap: Create a Connected App once in Anypoint Access Management (UI) with the 'Application Creator' scope, then paste its client_id/client_secret as env vars.
auth_model: Bearer token minted via client_credentials grant from a pre-provisioned Connected App (client_id/secret from env)
gates: Connected App must have 'Application Creator' scope on the right organization
org membership (ANYPOINT_ORG_ID must be an org the app can manage)
idempotency: On 409 (or 400 mentioning 'already/exist') it lists the org's applications and returns the existing one matching --name.
scope_model: Anypoint Connected App scopes (e.g. 'Application Creator'); optional apiVersionId binds a contract to an API instance
verify_notes: Verify the applications list response envelope for your org — it varies by control-plane version (.applications, .data, or bare array).
quirks: Regional control planes: default anypoint.mulesoft.com, EU at eu1.anypoint.mulesoft.com via ANYPOINT_BASE_URL; can also create an API contract via apiVersionId query param.

bootstrap: Create an integration by hand at notion.so/my-integrations and copy its secret (ntn_/secret_); for --oauth mode also create a public integration and register the 127.0.0.1:8765/callback redirect URI.
auth_model: Default: static internal integration secret as Bearer; --oauth: browser authorization-code flow with local callback + Basic client_id:client_secret token exchange (no PKCE); every request needs Notion-Version header
idempotency: Not applicable — it verifies/exchanges tokens rather than creating apps; no already-exists handling.
quirks: Meta-case: NO public API to create an integration or mint its token; two modes (internal secret verify vs public-integration OAuth); mandatory Notion-Version header on every request; warns if token resolves to a person not a bot.

bootstrap: Obtain an Okta admin API token (SSWS) or an OAuth2 access token with the okta.clients.manage scope for your org, and know your org base URL.
auth_model: Pasted admin token in Authorization header — sent as 'SSWS <token>' or auto-detected as 'Bearer <jwt>' if the token has two dots
credential_ttl_rotation: client_secret is only returned in the CREATE response (RFC 7591 behavior); for an existing client the secret isn't retrievable — rotate it in the Admin Console or via the client API.
gates: token must have okta.clients.manage scope or be a Super Admin SSWS token
idempotency: Names aren't unique so a true 409 is uncommon; if create fails it falls back to GET /oauth2/v1/clients?q=<name> (startsWith) requiring an exact client_name match, printing client_id with secret marked unretrievable.
scope_model: OAuth/OIDC client metadata (grant_types, response_types, redirect_uris); admin token itself gated by okta.clients.manage scope
quirks: Per-org tenant base URL; dual auth-header scheme (SSWS vs Bearer) inferred from token shape; client names are not unique in Okta so dedupe is best-effort; two error envelopes (DCR error/error_description vs management errorCode/errorSummary).

bootstrap: Manually create a REST app in the Developer Dashboard (developer.paypal.com -> Apps & Credentials -> Create App) and copy the client ID and secret.
auth_model: HTTP Basic (client_id:client_secret) client_credentials grant to mint a Bearer access token
credential_ttl_rotation: Token response includes expires_in; secret is never echoed (printed as '(provided via PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET; not echoed)').
gates: sandbox vs live credentials are not interchangeable
idempotency: Not applicable — no app creation; just mints a token each run.
scope_model: OAuth scope string returned in token response; probe treats 403 as valid-token-but-missing-product-scope
verify_notes: Probe endpoint availability depends on the app's granted scopes; catalogs/products chosen as broadly available.
quirks: Explicit bucket 'c': NO public API to create a REST app or read back client_id/secret; two env hosts (api-m.paypal.com live, api-m.sandbox.paypal.com sandbox); optional --probe verification call.

bootstrap: PingOne: pre-provision a 'worker' application and collect its client_id/secret plus the environment UUID; PingFederate (--pf): have a reachable PF base URL with DCR enabled (and an initial access token if required).
auth_model: PingOne: Basic worker client_credentials exchange for a Bearer Management API token (or pre-minted PINGONE_TOKEN); PingFederate: RFC 7591 DCR, anonymous or Bearer initial access token
gates: PingFederate DCR must be enabled in OAuth Settings -> Client Settings, or requires an initial access token
idempotency: PingOne returns 400 UNIQUENESS_VIOLATION on duplicate name; script then lists apps with SCIM filter name eq "..." and returns the existing one (secret re-read from /secret endpoint); PF path has no dedupe.
scope_model: OIDC client metadata (grantTypes/responseTypes/tokenEndpointAuthMethod) on PingOne apps; RFC 7591 metadata + scope 'openid' on PF
verify_notes: Verify the secret-read endpoint's vendor media type application/vnd.pingidentity.secret.read+json; plain application/json accepted in practice (script sends both).
quirks: Two front doors in one script (PingOne Management API default, PingFederate RFC 7591 DCR via --pf); regional api./auth. domain pairs (com/eu/ca/asia); PingOne's OIDC client_id IS the application id; secret read back via separate /secret endpoint; 404 on secret means public client.

bootstrap: Manually generate a personal API key in the Postman app (Settings -> Account settings -> API keys -> Generate API Key) and export it as POSTMAN_API_KEY.
auth_model: Personal API key (PAT-equivalent) sent in the X-Api-Key header
gates: 300 requests/minute rate limit (429 messaging)
idempotency: With --create-workspace it lists workspaces first and returns an existing same-named workspace instead of erroring.
verify_notes: /me wraps owner info under `user` and usage under `operations`; POST /workspaces expects { workspace: { name, type, description } }; fields read defensively.
quirks: The honest meta-case: an API-tooling company with no endpoint to mint its own key; script only verifies identity and optionally creates a workspace to prove write access — it mints no new credential.

bootstrap: Create (or reuse) a connected app in an org with http://127.0.0.1:8765/callback in its OAuth callback URLs, and supply its consumer key (secret optional if PKCE-only).
auth_model: Browser OAuth 2.0 web server flow (authorization_code + PKCE S256) with local callback server; client_secret included in token exchange when present
credential_ttl_rotation: refresh_token printed if granted (default scope 'api refresh_token'); consumer secret can only be revealed/rotated in Setup or via admin Connect REST credentials endpoint.
gates: consumer secret retrieval and ConnectedApp creation require admin permissions ('Customize Application'/'Modify Metadata')
redirect URI must be pre-registered on the connected app
idempotency: Not applicable — authorizes against an existing connected app; no create/already-exists path implemented.
verify_notes: Tooling API ConnectedApplication query exposes only metadata, never the consumerSecret; Salesforce never returns the secret over data/Tooling APIs.
quirks: Header documents but does not implement the full programmatic ConnectedApp create path (Metadata API deploy or Tooling sObject + admin-only Connect REST credentials read-back); tenant-specific login hosts (login/test.salesforce.com/My Domain); token response carries instance_url.

bootstrap: Create an app by hand in the Partner Dashboard, copy its API key/secret, and add http://127.0.0.1:8765/callback to the app's Allowed redirection URL(s).
auth_model: Browser OAuth 2.0 authorization code grant per store (no PKCE — client_secret is the credential), with HMAC-SHA256 callback verification and local callback server
credential_ttl_rotation: Default offline token has no expiry; --online requests a per-user expiring token (expires_in, associated_user, possible refresh_token).
gates: merchant must approve the grant screen for their store
redirect_uri must be allowlisted in the Partner Dashboard
idempotency: Not applicable — each run re-runs the grant for a shop; no app-exists handling (apps are created manually).
scope_model: Comma-separated Admin API access scopes (e.g. read_products,read_orders)
quirks: OAuth endpoints live on the per-store *.myshopify.com host, not a central host; callback carries an HMAC over sorted query params that must be verified with the app secret; credential is a shop access token (X-Shopify-Access-Token header), not a client_id/secret pair; offline vs online token duality.

bootstrap: Generate an App Configuration Token once at api.slack.com/apps ('Your App Configuration Tokens' -> Generate Token) and paste it (optionally with its refresh token) via env vars.
auth_model: Pasted app configuration access token (xoxe.xoxp-...) as a form param; optional auto-rotate via tooling.tokens.rotate using the xoxe- refresh token
credential_ttl_rotation: Config access tokens expire after 12h; setting SLACK_CONFIG_REFRESH_TOKEN makes the script rotate first (Slack rotates both tokens) and print the new pair to stderr; app secrets are only revealed at create time.
gates: workspace app limits (managed_app_limit_reached / failed_creating_app)
rate limiting (ratelimited)
org-level config tokens need --team team_id
idempotency: No auto-dedupe on create; the 'already have an app' path is explicit --update <app_id>, which updates the manifest but does NOT re-print secrets (view them on the app's Basic Information page).
scope_model: App manifest (display_information, settings, oauth_config with redirect_urls) — permissions expressed declaratively in the manifest
quirks: Slack Web API always returns HTTP 200 with an {ok:boolean} envelope, so errors are in-band codes; the config token is bound to a user+workspace (not an app) and can manage many apps; create returns four distinct secrets in one shot.

bootstrap: Create an application manually in the Square Developer Dashboard, copy the application ID + secret, and register http://127.0.0.1:8765/callback as the OAuth redirect URL for the matching environment.
auth_model: Browser OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow, confidential client (client_secret in JSON token exchange, no PKCE), local callback server accepting GET or POST callbacks; Square-Version header pinned
credential_ttl_rotation: Token response includes expires_at and a refresh_token for renewal.
gates: merchant must approve authorization
redirect URL must be pre-registered per environment
idempotency: Not applicable — no app creation; each run performs a fresh authorization for a merchant.
scope_model: Space-delimited UPPER_SNAKE permission scopes (e.g. MERCHANT_PROFILE_READ, PAYMENTS_READ)
quirks: Separate hosts per environment (connect.squareup.com vs connect.squareupsandbox.com); no application-creation API; durable credential is the per-merchant access_token+refresh_token, not a minted secret; identity verified via GET /v2/merchants/me (needs MERCHANT_PROFILE_READ).

bootstrap: Copy a secret key (sk_test_/sk_live_ or rk_) from the Stripe Dashboard at dashboard.stripe.com/apikeys — there is no API to create it.
auth_model: Secret key as Authorization: Bearer (Basic with key-as-username is the documented equivalent); form-encoded bodies
credential_ttl_rotation: Error messaging covers api_key_expired ('roll a new one in the Dashboard'); no TTL on the key itself.
gates: restricted keys (rk_) may lack permissions for some actions
new Connect accounts must complete onboarding before they can transact
idempotency: Not applicable — identity verification is read-only; --create-account creates a new connected account each run with no dedupe.
scope_model: Key tiers: unrestricted secret (sk_) vs restricted key (rk_) with per-resource permissions; livemode vs test mode
quirks: No public endpoint to mint your own secret/restricted key; the one programmatic onboarding path is Stripe Connect connected accounts (express/standard/custom), whose own restricted key must still be minted in the Dashboard afterward.

bootstrap: Admin mode: have a Stytch project and copy its project_id + secret; DCR mode (--dcr): only the project_id is needed — no secret.
auth_model: Admin: HTTP Basic base64(project_id:secret) against the Management endpoint; --dcr: unauthenticated RFC 7591 Dynamic Client Registration (project id in URL path)
credential_ttl_rotation: A confidential client_secret is shown EXACTLY ONCE — save it immediately.
idempotency: Management mode has no already-exists short-circuit (Stytch does not dedupe confidential clients); DCR with third_party_public + auth method 'none' dedupes by metadata per the comments.
scope_model: client_type taxonomy: first_party | first_party_public | third_party | third_party_public; token_endpoint_auth_method none vs client_secret_basic
verify_notes: Verify the DCR path against your project's custom authentication domain — canonical form is https://{projectDomain}/v1/oauth2/register; script uses the legacy /v1/public/{projectId}/... form.
quirks: Dual-bucket single script: authenticated Management create (b) and truly anonymous public DCR (a) built for the agentic story; env-split hosts test.stytch.com vs api.stytch.com; public clients get no secret (use PKCE).

bootstrap: Copy your Account SID (AC...) and Auth Token from the Twilio Console dashboard.
auth_model: HTTP Basic auth (username = Account SID, password = Auth Token) against the 2010-04-01 REST API with form-encoded bodies
credential_ttl_rotation: The API Key secret is returned exactly once at creation and is never retrievable afterward; API Keys are described as long-lived.
idempotency: Lists Keys.json by friendly name first; if a same-named key exists it exits with code 2 (secret can't be reprinted) unless --force mints a new key/secret.
quirks: --subaccount provisions a fresh isolated tenant first (subaccounts authenticate with their OWN sid + auth_token) and mints the key inside it; distinctive exit code 2 for the exists-but-secret-unrecoverable case.

bootstrap: Have a reachable Tyk Dashboard, copy your Dashboard API Access Credentials secret from your profile, and have an existing OAuth-enabled API's api_id (plus normally a policy id).
auth_model: Raw Dashboard secret in the Authorization header (no scheme word) on every Dashboard API request
credential_ttl_rotation: Tyk does not return the original secret when listing existing clients — reused clients may come back without a secret; pass --new/omit --reuse to mint fresh.
gates: Dashboard user must be able to manage the API
target API must have OAuth 2.0 enabled
clients normally need to be bound to an access policy or the Dashboard may reject
idempotency: Opt-in via --reuse: lists existing OAuth clients under the API and reuses the first with a client_id (secret possibly omitted); otherwise always creates.
scope_model: Access expressed by binding the client to a Tyk access Policy (policy_id), not scope strings
verify_notes: Verify the client-list response envelope against your Dashboard version — may be a bare array, {clients:[...]}, or {oauth_clients:[...]}.
quirks: Non-standard auth header (raw secret, no Bearer/Basic scheme); OAuth clients are nested under a specific API's api_id and bound to a policy; works for self-managed or Tyk Cloud control-plane URLs; client field casing varies (client_id/clientId/ClientID).

bootstrap: Mint a secret API key (sk_...) in the WorkOS Dashboard under API Keys — there is no public endpoint to create this root key.
auth_model: Secret API key as Authorization: Bearer sk_... on every request
gates: key environment split (sk_test_ vs sk_live_) controls what it can touch
rate limiting (429 messaging)
idempotency: Searches GET /organizations?search=<name> first and returns an exact-name match instead of creating; also retries the search on 409 (duplicate external_id).
quirks: No /me endpoint — key verified by listing one organization; the created resource is an Organization (tenant), not an OAuth client; header explicitly flags DCR-adjacent features (customer API keys via POST /organizations/{id}/api_keys, Connect M2M apps) as out of scope.

bootstrap: Run/reach a WSO2 API Manager deployment and supply an operator username/password (or a pre-obtained DevPortal token via WSO2_TOKEN).
auth_model: Chain: RFC 7591 DCR with HTTP Basic username:password -> password-grant token (Basic clientId:clientSecret, scopes apim:subscribe apim:app_manage) -> Bearer for DevPortal REST calls
credential_ttl_rotation: generate-keys requests validityTime: 3600 for issued tokens; existing keys are read back rather than rotated.
gates: operator credentials on a deployment you run
token needs apim:subscribe apim:app_manage scopes
idempotency: App create 409 -> looks up the application by exact name and reuses it; generate-keys 409 -> reads back existing keys for that keyType; DCR itself returns 200 for an already-registered client name.
scope_model: DevPortal apps bound to a throttlingPolicy (default Unlimited) with PRODUCTION/SANDBOX key types and per-app grantTypesToBeSupported; REST API access gated by apim: scope strings
verify_notes: Verify the DCR path version (default v0.17) per install; self-signed certs are common so WSO2_INSECURE_TLS=1 disables TLS verification (opt-in, with warning).
quirks: Fully scriptable four-step chain on self-hosted software (DCR -> password grant -> create app -> generate-keys); two layers of credentials (the CLI's own DCR client vs the app's consumerKey/Secret); uses deprecated-style password grant; keyManager 'Resident Key Manager'.

bootstrap: Create a Server-to-Server OAuth app once in the Zoom App Marketplace dashboard (web UI only) and copy account_id, client_id, client_secret from the App credentials screen.
auth_model: HTTP Basic (client_id:client_secret) POST to the token endpoint with grant_type=account_credentials in the query string — fully headless, no browser
credential_ttl_rotation: Tokens expire in 3600s (1 hour) and there is NO refresh token — you simply mint a new one each hour.
idempotency: Not applicable — mints a fresh token each run; no resources created.
scope_model: Scopes granted to the S2S app in the Marketplace dashboard, echoed as a scope string in the token response
quirks: Token params ride in the query string while Zoom still expects a form content-type header; token response returns a per-cluster api_url that the verify call honors instead of assuming api.zoom.us.

bootstrap: Generate a Zuplo Developer API key by hand in the Zuplo Portal (account settings -> 'Zuplo API Keys') and note your account name and API key bucket name from Portal Settings.
auth_model: Long-lived Developer API key (account-scoped admin) sent as Authorization: Bearer against the Zuplo Developer API
credential_ttl_rotation: Key objects carry an optional expiresOn field; existing consumers' keys may come back masked/without a visible value.
idempotency: No documented 409 on duplicate, so 409/422/400 all trigger a list (include-api-keys=true, key-format=visible) to find and return the existing consumer's current key(s).
scope_model: Object model Bucket -> Consumer -> API Key(s); access shaped by bucket/consumer plus tags/metadata, not scope strings
verify_notes: Project-local /v1/consumers routes are operator-defined; the documented surface is dev.zuplo.com (ZUPLO_API_URL can override).
quirks: Consumer names must match ^[a-z0-9-]{1,128}$ (script slugifies); the 'credential' is a metered API-key consumer in a bucket rather than an OAuth client; keys can be listed with visible values via key-format=visible, unusual among providers.
